FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study at Year 2 and Year 3 follow-up; 3,687 early adolescents (mean age 11.96 years); NIH-funded multi-site cohort.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective longitudinal analysis: Fitbit-measured sleep parameters at Year 2, sensation-seeking assessed at Year 2, mature screen usage assessed at Year 3; reports β coefficients and 95% CIs for prospective associations.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article published in Sleep journal, October 2024 advance access.

FT_IC2.evidence: Outcome is mature-rated video games and R-rated movies (content rating), not phone/SM-specific modality. Per §2.4 BINDING clarification: "Mature-rated / R-rated content alone does not qualify." Step 1 finds no phone-time or named-SM-platform estimates. Step 2: composite items are "mature video games" and "R-rated movies" — content rating is not modality and fails IC2.

notes: Paper reports prospective associations between sleep, sensation-seeking, and mature screen use, but mature-content-only exposure fails IC2 regardless of other-side individual-trait measures.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, Release 5.1, Year-4 follow-up; 495 adolescents with EARS app data from Android phones.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: compares passive smartphone tracking (EARS app) with self-reported screen time and examines associations with externalizing/internalizing symptoms and SM/game addiction scales; reports β coefficients and 95% CIs.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in npj Mental Health Research, published online May 10, 2025.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 success: Reports social media use paired with individual-trait outcomes. Main results: self-reported social media β = 0.06 (95% CI 0.01–0.11) with externalizing symptoms; EARS social media β = 0.07 (95% CI 0.03–0.12) with externalizing symptoms. Also social media correlated with SM addiction (SMAQ: self-report β = 0.15, 95% CI 0.10–0.20; EARS β = 0.06, 95% CI 0.01–0.11). Self-reported social media also correlated with internalizing symptoms (β = 0.05, 95% CI 0.01–0.09). Phone/SM variable qualifies: "social media use" (named platform, hours per day). Other-side qualifies: externalizing/internalizing behaviors (individual-trait mental health), not demographic.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported for SM↔individual-trait relationships across main text and figures: externalizing symptoms (β = 0.06–0.12), internalizing symptoms (β = 0.05), SM addiction (β = 0.05–0.15), video game addiction (β = 0.05).

FT_IC4.evidence: Any quantitative result counts per §2.5. Multiple β coefficients reported with 95% CIs and p-values for SM↔behavioral/addiction outcomes in main results and supplementary analyses.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design: paper analyzes associations between SMA and behavioral problems/addiction at Year-4 follow-up without longitudinal SM-as-DV-only structure. Cross-sectional carve-out applies.
notes: Paper directly measures social media use (self-report and objective EARS app) and correlates with externalizing, internalizing, and addiction outcomes. Includes additional gaming and vocabulary variables, but core SM↔trait relationship qualifies.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, baseline to Year 2 follow-up (2016–2020); N = 10,757 children ages 10–14 years from 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective longitudinal analysis: mixed-effect models with robust standard errors examining associations between baseline parental monitoring and family conflict scores with repeated measures of total and modality-specific screen time at Years 1 and 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in Acta Paediatrica, published 2024;113:2452–2458.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimates reported (e.g., no "time on Instagram" or "texting hours"). Paper analyzes "total screen time" and six modalities separately (television, YouTube videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media). Step 2: Composite "total screen time" bundles television with phone/SM modalities. Per §2.4 BINDING: "Multi-device aggregated time fails Step 2... unless a per-device breakout (cellphone alone) exists somewhere." Television is explicitly non-phone/non-SM (contextual screening device, not personal communication/social platform). Composite includes television without separate breakout; therefore fails Step 2. Additionally, other-side (parental monitoring, family conflict) are family-context/parenting-practice variables, NOT individual-trait-level per §2.2 definition.

notes: Paper examines parental monitoring and family conflict as predictors of screen time across six modalities. Fails IC2 on two grounds: (1) no phone/SM-specific variable isolated; (2) composite includes television (non-phone/SM) without breakout; (3) other-side is family-context/parenting (not individual-trait-level). Parenting practices and family conflict are contextual, not individual-trait outcomes per criteria.
